entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: The word that stands settled as the expressible of that

entities.name: vacanarūpa
entities.cite: a form distinct from it is discerned

entities.name: śabdatattva
entities.cite: the duality belongs to the word's essence

axioms.name: abhidheya
axioms.cite: the named word is the abhidheya, the expressible

axioms.name: anya
axioms.cite: the uttered token is other than the settled type it presents

axioms.name: dvaita
axioms.cite: the duality belongs to the word's essence

denials.cite: They are not; the token heard in the classroom is a dated acoustic event, the form taught is none of them

claims.name: vivichyate
claims.cite: in the uttering of it too, a form distinct from it is discerned

rejected_readings.label: citation_collapse
rejected_readings.rendering: When we mention a word, the distinction between sound and word disappears; in citation, sound and word are simply identical.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly discerns a form distinct from the word in utterance. Treating sound and word as identical violates the core relation (anya) that the verse establishes. The commentary rejects this naive picture.

rejected_readings.label: form_as_accident
rejected_readings.rendering: The utterance-form is merely an accidental property of the word, not fundamentally distinct from it.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's vivichyate (discerned apart) and anya (distinct) establish a necessary relation between word and utterance-form. Treating the form as an accidental property rather than a distinct manifestation demotes the duality below the essential level that the commentary emphasizes: 'the duality belongs to the word's essence'.

notes: The contract encodes the duality between word-type and utterance-token that recurs even in metalinguistic contexts (mentioning words). The 'duality' property belongs to śabdatattva (word-essence) rather than being circumstantial; this places the essential two-sidedness at the absolute level, consistent with Bharṭhari's philosophy.
